sections.markdown:
# Metaobject Protocol Teaser

In CLOS, *classes are themselves objects* — instances of a class called `standard-class`. *Generic functions* are instances of `standard-generic-function`. *Methods* are instances of `standard-method`. The protocol that lets you look at, and customise, these objects is the **Metaobject Protocol** (MOP).

The MOP is described in *The Art of the Metaobject Protocol* (Kiczales et al., 1991). It is not part of the ANSI standard, but every major implementation provides it. SBCL exposes it in the package `sb-mop`. The portability shim everyone uses is the library `closer-mop` (`closer-mop:class-precedence-list`, `closer-mop:class-direct-slots`, etc.).

For everyday work you only need a few standard CL accessors:

* `(class-of obj)` → the class metaobject of the object.
* `(class-name class)` → its name (a symbol).
* `(find-class 'name)` → look up a class by name.
* `(type-of obj)`, `(typep obj 'type)`, `(subtypep 'a 'b)` — type queries.

With the MOP you can:

* Walk slot definitions and build serialisers.
* Define metaclasses that, say, persist instances or auto-add a logging accessor.
* Intercept method addition.

We won't go deeper here; this lesson just gives you the vocabulary. If you ever read a sentence like "specialise on the metaclass" without flinching, you've made it.

sections.explain: Every instance carries its class; CLASS-NAME pulls the symbol back out.
FIND-CLASS and CLASS-OF give the same metaobject.
TYPEP is the runtime type test.
SUBTYPEP returns T plus a confidence flag (T = answer is reliable). We only see the primary value.

sections.prompt: Define a class GADGET with no slots and no superclasses. Define CLASS-NAME-OF that takes any instance and returns the name (symbol) of its class.
